terms.def: Geometric Brownian Motion: dS = μS dt + σS dW; log-normal price process with constant drift and volatility.
Expected rate of return per unit time; the deterministic part of the GBM.
Standard deviation of log-returns per unit time; the random part; annualised σ = daily σ × √252.
Continuous-time random walk; increments dW ~ N(0, dt) — the source of randomness in GBM.
If S follows GBM, then log(S) is normally distributed — ensures prices remain positive.
Probability measure under which all assets grow at the risk-free rate; used for derivative pricing.

eli3: Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM) is the standard mathematical model for stock prices. It says stock prices grow at a constant expected rate (drift) and experience random, normally distributed shocks (diffusion). The 'geometric' part ensures prices can never go negative — which makes physical sense for stocks.

The GBM equation: dS = μS dt + σS dW, where S is the stock price, μ is the expected return (drift), σ is the volatility, and dW is a Brownian motion increment (a tiny random shock). The key feature: percentage changes in price are normally distributed, which means log prices follow a random walk with drift — log-normal distribution for prices.

GBM is the foundation of the Black-Scholes option pricing model. It's a simplification of reality (real prices have fat tails, volatility clustering, and jumps), but it's analytically tractable and provides closed-form solutions that are foundational to derivatives pricing and risk management.

whenWhy.title: Deriving the log-normal distribution of stock prices
whenWhy.body: From dS = μS dt + σS dW, apply Ito's lemma to f(S) = ln(S): d(ln S) = (μ - σ²/2) dt + σ dW. This shows log prices follow a Brownian motion with drift (μ - σ²/2) and diffusion σ. Integrating: ln(S_T) = ln(S_0) + (μ - σ²/2)T + σ√T × Z, where Z ~ N(0,1). Therefore: S_T = S_0 × exp[(μ - σ²/2)T + σ√T × Z]. S_T is log-normally distributed: ln(S_T/S_0) ~ N((μ - σ²/2)T, σ²T). Key: E[S_T] = S_0 × e^{μT} (grows at rate μ); median of S_T = S_0 × e^{(μ-σ²/2)T} (grows slower due to the Jensen inequality correction -σ²/2). The -σ²/2 term is the Ito correction — it appears because variance in geometric growth reduces the median outcome.

whenWhy.title: Monte Carlo simulation of stock price paths
whenWhy.body: GBM Monte Carlo simulation: generate N paths of S_t using the discretised GBM: S_{t+Δt} = S_t × exp[(μ - σ²/2)Δt + σ√Δt × Z_t], where Z_t ~ N(0,1) iid. Steps: (1) Choose Δt (e.g., daily = 1/252 years); (2) Generate N×T matrix of N(0,1) random numbers; (3) Recursively compute S_t along each path; (4) Use the distribution of S_T across paths to price options or compute VaR. Applications: (1) European option pricing (matches Black-Scholes analytically); (2) Exotic option pricing with path-dependent payoffs; (3) Portfolio risk simulation; (4) Corporate scenario analysis. Key: use variance reduction techniques (antithetic variates, quasi-random sequences) to improve convergence efficiency.

whenWhy.title: Parameter estimation from historical data
whenWhy.body: Estimate GBM parameters from historical returns: μ̂ = mean(daily return) × 252 (annualise); σ̂ = std(daily return) × √252 (annualise). From log returns: r_t = ln(S_t/S_{t-1}) ~ N((μ - σ²/2)/252, σ²/252). So mean(r_t) = (μ - σ²/2)/252 and var(r_t) = σ²/252. Estimation from daily returns (T = 252 observations): σ is estimated precisely (σ grows with √T; more observations help); μ is estimated very imprecisely (standard error ≈ σ/√T ≈ 20%/√252 ≈ 1.3% daily, implying ±26% annual confidence interval). This 'estimation problem' is fundamental: we can estimate volatility reliably from daily data but cannot estimate expected return reliably even from decades of data. Implication: portfolio optimisation is more reliable using risk (σ) than expected return (μ).

deepDiveIntro: The GBM stochastic differential equation states that instantaneous price changes equal a deterministic drift term plus a random diffusion term, both proportional to the current price. Applying Ito's lemma to the log of the price adds an extra correction term that arises because the squared increment of Brownian motion equals the time increment rather than zero as in ordinary calculus — the fundamental distinction between stochastic and standard calculus. The closed-form solution shows the price at any future time is the starting price multiplied by an exponential whose exponent is normally distributed. For multiple correlated assets, Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix converts independent random increments into correlated ones.
formula: S_T = S_0 · exp[(μ − σ²/2)T + σW_T]

deepDive.heading: GBM Limitations: Fat Tails, Volatility Clustering, and Jumps
deepDive.body: GBM assumes constant volatility, normally distributed returns, no price jumps, and continuous trading. Real markets violate all four assumptions. Daily S&P 500 returns have excess kurtosis around twenty to thirty, meaning extreme moves occur far more often than GBM predicts. Volatility clusters in time rather than staying constant, and prices can jump discontinuously on news events. Extensions such as the Heston stochastic-volatility model, Merton's jump-diffusion model, and GARCH processes each address one or more of these failures at the cost of requiring numerical rather than closed-form pricing. [1]

deepDive.heading: Multi-Asset GBM and Correlation Dynamics
deepDive.body: Multiple assets can be modeled as correlated GBMs by linking their Brownian increments through a pairwise correlation structure. A portfolio of such assets does not itself follow GBM, so Monte Carlo simulation is the practical tool for risk calculations. The main vulnerability is that historical correlations are measured during normal times but tend to spike toward one in crises, exactly when diversification is most needed. Copulas with tail dependence better capture this behavior. The 2008 crisis illustrated this clearly: equity markets that historically had modest correlations moved nearly in lockstep, turning expected diversified volatility into realized volatility far higher. [2]

deepDive.heading: Risk-Neutral GBM and Option Pricing
deepDive.body: For derivative pricing, GBM is recast under the risk-neutral measure, where all assets are assumed to grow at the risk-free rate rather than their actual expected return. This substitution is justified by a no-arbitrage argument and eliminates the need to estimate unobservable expected returns. The result is the Black-Scholes formula for European option prices, which depends only on the current stock price, strike, maturity, risk-free rate, and implied volatility — all market-observable quantities. The theoretical foundation for this change of measure is Girsanov's theorem. [1]

takeaways: GBM: dS = μS dt + σS dW; log returns are normally distributed; prices are log-normally distributed. [1]
Ito correction: d(ln S) = (μ - σ²/2)dt + σdW; the -σ²/2 term reduces geometric mean relative to arithmetic mean. [1]
Estimate σ precisely from historical data; μ is essentially unknowable from any finite sample. [1]
GBM fails on fat tails, volatility clustering, and jumps — real markets have kurtosis ~20-30. [1]
Risk-neutral GBM: replace μ with r for options pricing (Girsanov's theorem); eliminates need to estimate μ. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why does GBM fail to capture the volatility smile in options markets?
edgeCases.a: GBM with constant σ implies a single implied volatility for all options on the same underlying — a flat volatility surface. In reality, market-implied volatilities vary by strike and maturity, forming a 'volatility smile' (or skew). The skew (put implied vol > call implied vol at same distance from ATM) reflects: (1) fat left tail — markets price in higher probability of large declines than GBM predicts; (2) leverage effect — stocks fall faster on bad news; (3) crash risk premium — put options are expensive because they provide portfolio insurance. Extensions: local volatility models (Dupire 1994) make σ a deterministic function of S and t to match the smile exactly; stochastic volatility models (Heston) make σ itself random, generating volatility smiles naturally. Both are used in practice depending on the application.

For dividend-paying stocks, modify GBM: dS = (μ - q)S dt + σS dW, where q is the continuous dividend yield. The stock price falls by q continuously (dividends are paid out, reducing intrinsic value). For discrete dividends: subtract the present value of expected dividends from S₀ before applying GBM. In Black-Scholes for dividend-paying stocks (Merton 1973): replace S₀ with S₀e^{-qT} in the formula. For equity index options: q is the index dividend yield (typically 1.5-2.5% for S&P 500). This adjustment matters especially for long-dated options where dividend yield has significant impact on pricing. Currencies: treat the foreign risk-free rate r_f as the 'dividend yield' — the Garman-Kohlhagen model extends Black-Scholes to FX options this way.

sources.label: Black, F. & Scholes, M. (1973) — The Pricing of Options and Corporate Liabilities, JPE
Hull, J.C. — Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives (10th ed.), Pearson
